content.text:
A field extension is a pair of fields, one contained in the other. The 
study of how fields extend and relate is the heart of Galois theory.

content.title: Field Extension
content.content:
A **field extension** is a field $E$ 
containing a subfield $F$. We write $E/F$ or $F \subseteq E$ and say 
"$E$ over $F$" or "$E$ extends $F$."

content.title: Degree of Extension
content.content:
The **degree** of $E/F$, denoted 
$[E:F]$, is the dimension of $E$ as a vector space over $F$:
\[
 [E:F] = \dim_F(E)
\]
If $[E:F]$ is finite, $E/F$ is a **finite extension**.

content.title: Basic extensions
content.problem:
- $[\C:\R] = 2$ with basis $\{1, i\}$

- $[\R:\Q] = \infty$ (uncountably infinite)

- $[\Q(\sqrt{2}):\Q] = 2$ with basis $\{1, \sqrt{2}\}$

- $[\F_{p^n}:\F_p] = n$

content.title: Simple Extension
content.content:
An extension $E/F$ is **simple** if 
$E = F(\alpha)$ for some $\alpha \in E$. We read this as "$F$ adjoin 
$\alpha$."

content.title: Adjoining elements
content.problem:
- $\Q(\sqrt{2}) = \{a + b\sqrt{2} : a, b \in \Q\}$

- $\Q(i) = \{a + bi : a, b \in \Q\}$

- $\Q(\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3})$ requires two generators

content.title: Tower Law (Degree Multiplicativity)
content.content:
If $F \subseteq E \subseteq K$ are fields with $[E:F]$ and $[K:E]$ 
finite, then:
\[
 [K:F] = [K:E] \cdot [E:F]
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $\{e_1, \ldots, e_m\}$ be a basis for $E/F$ and $\{k_1, \ldots, k_n\}$ 
be a basis for $K/E$. We claim $\{e_i k_j : 1 \leq i \leq m, 1 \leq j 
\leq n\}$ is a basis for $K/F$.

**Spanning:** Any $\alpha \in K$ can be written $\alpha = \sum_j 
a_j k_j$ with $a_j \in E$. Each $a_j = \sum_i b_{ij} e_i$ with 
$b_{ij} \in F$. Thus $\alpha = \sum_{i,j} b_{ij} e_i k_j$.

**Independence:** If $\sum_{i,j} b_{ij} e_i k_j = 0$ with 
$b_{ij} \in F$, then $\sum_j (\sum_i b_{ij} e_i) k_j = 0$. By 
independence of $\{k_j\}$ over $E$, $\sum_i b_{ij} e_i = 0$ for each $j$. 
By independence of $\{e_i\}$ over $F$, $b_{ij} = 0$ for all $i, j$.

items:
If $[E:F] = p$ (prime), prove $E = F(\alpha)$ for any 
 $\alpha \in E \setminus F$.

marginNotes.text:
The path of the righteous is like the 
morning sun, shining ever brighter till the full light of day.
